content.text: Atoms and molecules are the fundamental building blocks of matter. The existence of different kinds of matter is due to different atoms constituting them. This raises two fundamental questions: (i) What makes the atom of one element different from the atom of another element? (ii) Are atoms really indivisible, as proposed by Dalton, or are there smaller constituents inside the atom?

The major challenge before scientists at the end of the 19th century was to reveal the structure of the atom and explain its important properties. One of the first indications that atoms are not indivisible comes from studying static electricity and the conditions under which electricity is conducted by different substances.

content.title: Key Concept
content.text: The elucidation of atomic structure is based on a series of experiments conducted by different scientists, each contributing crucial insights about sub-atomic particles and their arrangement.

By 1900, it was known that the atom contained at least one sub-atomic particle: the electron, identified by J.J. Thomson through his experiments on cathode rays. This discovery proved that atoms are not indivisible as previously thought.

Before the electron was identified, E. Goldstein in 1886 discovered canal rays (positive radiations) in gas discharge tubes. These radiations were positively charged and ultimately led to the discovery of another sub-atomic particle called the proton. The proton had a charge equal in magnitude but opposite in sign to that of the electron, with a mass approximately 2000 times greater than the electron.

content.title: Representation and Properties
content.text: An electron is represented as 'e⁻' and a proton as 'p⁺'. The mass of a proton is taken as one unit and its charge as plus one. The mass of an electron is considered negligible and its charge is minus one.

It was observed that an atom is composed of protons and electrons, mutually balancing their charges to make the atom electrically neutral overall. Protons are located in the interior of the atom, while electrons can be easily removed. This raised the fundamental question: what sort of structure do these particles form within the atom?

content.title: Activity 4.1 - Discovering Charged Particles
procedure: A. Comb dry hair. Observe whether the comb then attracts small pieces of paper.
B. Rub a glass rod with a silk cloth and bring the rod near an inflated balloon. Observe what happens.
content.observation: In Activity A, the comb attracts paper pieces. In Activity B, the charged rod attracts or repels the balloon.
content.conclusion: When two objects are rubbed together, they become electrically charged. This charge comes from the transfer of electrons, proving that atoms contain charged particles.

content.text: J.J. Thomson proposed the first model of atomic structure after the discovery of electrons. He visualized the atom as being similar to a Christmas pudding, where electrons (like currants or dry fruits) are embedded in a sphere of positive charge. This can also be visualized as a watermelon, where the positive charge is spread uniformly like the red edible part, and electrons are studded throughout like seeds.

content.title: Thomson's Model Postulates
content.text: Thomson proposed that: (i) An atom consists of a positively charged sphere with electrons embedded in it. (ii) The negative and positive charges are equal in magnitude, making the atom as a whole electrically neutral.

Although Thomson's model successfully explained the electrical neutrality of atoms, it could not explain the results of experiments conducted by other scientists. This limitation led to the proposal of alternative atomic models.

Ernest Rutherford designed a groundbreaking experiment to determine how electrons are arranged within an atom. In this experiment, fast-moving alpha (α)-particles (doubly-charged helium ions with mass 4 u) were made to fall on a very thin gold foil (approximately 1000 atoms thick). Alpha particles have considerable energy due to their mass, and Rutherford expected them to be deflected by sub-atomic particles in the gold atoms, but he did not expect large deflections since alpha particles were much heavier than protons.

content.title: Experimental Observations
content.text: The alpha-particle scattering experiment gave totally unexpected results: (i) Most of the fast-moving α-particles passed straight through the gold foil without deflection. (ii) Some α-particles were deflected by the foil by small angles. (iii) Surprisingly, one out of every 12,000 particles appeared to rebound (180° deflection).

Rutherford famously remarked about this result: 'This result was almost as incredible as if you fire a 15-inch shell at a piece of tissue paper and it comes back and hits you.' To understand the implications, consider an analogy: when stones are thrown at a wall, each stone produces a sound. However, when stones are thrown at a barbed-wire fence, most pass through the gaps without producing sound. Similarly, the gaps in atomic structure allowed most α-particles to pass through.

content.title: Rutherford's Conclusions
content.text: From the α-particle scattering experiment, Rutherford concluded: (i) Most of the space inside the atom is empty because most α-particles passed through without deflection. (ii) Very few particles were deflected, indicating that positive charge occupies very little space. (iii) A small fraction underwent 180° deflection, indicating all positive charge and mass were concentrated in a very small volume. The radius of the nucleus is about 10⁵ times less than the radius of the atom.

topics.topic_name: Rutherford's Nuclear Model of the Atom

content.title: Nuclear Model Features
content.text: On the basis of his experiment, Rutherford put forward the nuclear model with the following features: (i) There is a positively charged centre called the nucleus. Nearly all the mass of an atom resides in the nucleus. (ii) Electrons revolve around the nucleus in circular paths. (iii) The size of the nucleus is very small compared to the size of the atom.

Rutherford's model explained the experimental observations and showed that atoms have a concentrated central core (nucleus) surrounded by a large empty space. This model was revolutionary as it contradicted Thomson's model of uniformly distributed positive charge.

content.title: Drawbacks of Rutherford's Model
content.text: The revolution of electrons in circular orbits around the nucleus is not expected to be stable. Any particle in circular orbit undergoes acceleration. During acceleration, charged particles radiate energy continuously. Thus, the revolving electron would lose energy and eventually fall into the nucleus. If this were true, atoms should be highly unstable, and matter would not exist in the form we observe. However, we know atoms are quite stable, indicating a flaw in Rutherford's model.
Niels Bohr proposed a new atomic model to overcome the objections raised against Rutherford's model. His model successfully explained the stability of atoms and predicted their properties.

content.title: Bohr's Postulates
content.text: In order to overcome the objections raised against Rutherford's model, Niels Bohr put forward the following postulates: (i) Only certain special orbits known as discrete orbits of electrons are allowed inside the atom. (ii) While revolving in discrete orbits, electrons do not radiate energy.

According to Bohr's model, electrons are distributed in different shells or energy levels around the nucleus. These orbits are represented by the letters K, L, M, N, ... or by the numbers n = 1, 2, 3, 4, ... where n represents the shell number or energy level index. Each shell can accommodate a maximum number of electrons determined by the Bohr-Bury scheme.

content.title: Key Advantage
content.text: Bohr's model successfully explained the stability of atoms by proposing that electrons occupy discrete energy levels and do not lose energy while orbiting the nucleus. Atoms are stable because electrons remain in their allowed discrete orbits. The atomic properties depend on the electronic configuration in these shells.

In 1932, J. Chadwick discovered another sub-atomic particle which had no electrical charge and a mass nearly equal to that of a proton. This particle was named the neutron and is represented as 'n'. Neutrons are present in the nucleus of all atoms except hydrogen.

content.title: The Three Sub-Atomic Particles
content.text: The atom consists of three fundamental sub-atomic particles: (i) Electrons (e⁻) - negatively charged particles, mass negligible compared to protons. (ii) Protons (p⁺) - positively charged particles, mass = 1 unit. (iii) Neutrons (n) - neutral particles with no charge, mass ≈ 1 unit. The mass of an electron is approximately 1/2000 times the mass of a proton or neutron.

The mass of an atom is given by the sum of the masses of protons and neutrons present in its nucleus, as electrons contribute negligibly to the atomic mass. This is because protons and neutrons are approximately 2000 times heavier than electrons.

The distribution of electrons into different orbits or shells of an atom was suggested by Bohr and Bury. This scheme follows specific rules to determine the maximum number of electrons that can occupy each shell.

content.title: Rule 1: Maximum Electrons per Shell
content.text: The maximum number of electrons present in a shell is given by the formula 2n², where n is the orbit number or energy level index (n = 1, 2, 3, ...). Therefore: K-shell (n=1): 2×1² = 2 electrons; L-shell (n=2): 2×2² = 8 electrons; M-shell (n=3): 2×3² = 18 electrons; N-shell (n=4): 2×4² = 32 electrons.

content.title: Rule 2: Outermost Shell Limit
content.text: The maximum number of electrons that can be accommodated in the outermost shell is 8. This is the octet rule, which applies to all atoms. An outermost shell with 8 electrons is said to be stable and complete.

content.title: Rule 3: Step-wise Filling
content.text: Electrons are not accommodated in a given shell unless the inner shells are filled completely. In other words, shells are filled in a step-wise manner starting from the innermost shell. An inner shell must be filled before electrons are placed in the next outer shell.

Example 1: Distribution of electrons in Carbon (C): Carbon has 6 electrons. Following the Bohr-Bury scheme: K-shell (n=1): 2 electrons (maximum capacity 2) → filled. L-shell (n=2): 4 electrons (remaining electrons) → configuration is K(2), L(4) or 2,4.

Example 2: Distribution of electrons in Sodium (Na): Sodium has 11 electrons. K-shell: 2 electrons (filled). L-shell: 8 electrons (filled). M-shell: 1 electron. Configuration: K(2), L(8), M(1) or 2,8,1.

Example 3: Distribution of electrons in Chlorine (Cl): Chlorine has 17 electrons. K-shell: 2 electrons (filled). L-shell: 8 electrons (filled). M-shell: 7 electrons. Configuration: K(2), L(8), M(7) or 2,8,7.

content.title: Activity 4.2 - Electronic Configuration Model
procedure: Make a static atomic model displaying the electronic configuration of the first eighteen elements.
Use appropriate materials to represent shells and electrons.
Ensure correct placement of electrons according to the Bohr-Bury scheme.
content.observation: The models show progressive filling of shells as atomic number increases.
content.conclusion: The systematic distribution of electrons in shells follows predictable rules based on shell capacity and the step-wise filling principle.

topics.topic_name: Understanding Valency and Chemical Combining Capacity

content.text: The electrons present in the outermost shell of an atom are known as valence electrons. The valency of an element is the combining capacity of its atoms, representing the number of electrons an atom gains, loses, or shares to fill its outermost shell or achieve stability.

content.title: The Octet Rule
content.text: From the Bohr-Bury scheme, we know that the outermost shell of an atom can accommodate a maximum of 8 electrons. Atoms with a completely filled outermost shell containing 8 electrons show very little chemical activity and have zero valency. These are called inert or noble gas atoms. Helium, with 2 electrons in its outermost (and only) shell, is an exception and is also inert.

Atoms react and combine with other atoms to achieve a stable configuration, typically by obtaining an octet (8 electrons) in their outermost shell. This is done by sharing, gaining, or losing electrons. The number of electrons gained, lost, or shared to achieve an octet directly gives the valency of the element.

Example 1: Valency of Hydrogen (H): H has 1 electron in its outermost shell (K-shell). To achieve stability (like He with 2 electrons), H can lose 1 electron or gain 1 electron. Therefore, valency of H = 1.
Example 2: Valency of Lithium (Li) and Sodium (Na): Both have 1 electron in their outermost shell. They can easily lose this 1 electron to form stable cations (Li⁺, Na⁺). Therefore, valency = 1.
Example 3: Valency of Magnesium (Mg): Mg has 2 electrons in its outermost shell (M-shell). It can lose these 2 electrons to form Mg²⁺ ion. Therefore, valency of Mg = 2.
Example 4: Valency of Aluminum (Al): Al has 3 electrons in its outermost shell (M-shell). It can lose these 3 electrons to form Al³⁺ ion. Therefore, valency of Al = 3.

When the number of electrons in the outermost shell is close to the octet (more than 4), it becomes easier for the atom to gain electrons rather than lose electrons. In such cases, valency is determined by counting how many electrons are needed to complete the octet.

Example 5: Valency of Fluorine (F): F has 7 electrons in its outermost shell. It could have valency of 7 by losing all electrons, but it is much easier to gain 1 electron. By gaining 1 electron, F achieves an octet and becomes F⁻ ion. Therefore, valency of F = 1 (determined by 8 - 7 = 1).
Example 6: Valency of Oxygen (O): O has 6 electrons in its outermost shell. To achieve an octet, it needs to gain 2 electrons. Therefore, valency of O = 2 (determined by 8 - 6 = 2).
Example 7: Valency of Silicon (Si): Si has 4 electrons in its outermost shell. It can either lose 4 electrons or gain 4 electrons to stabilize. Therefore, valency of Si = 4.
Example 8: Valency of Carbon (C): C has 4 electrons in its outermost shell. To achieve stability, it can lose, gain, or share 4 electrons. Therefore, valency of C = 4.

content.title: Valency Rule for Determination
content.text: When outermost shell electrons ≤ 4: Valency = number of electrons in outermost shell (by losing/sharing). When outermost shell electrons > 4: Valency = 8 - number of electrons in outermost shell (by gaining/sharing). For example, N with 5 electrons: valency = 8 - 5 = 3; P with 5 electrons: valency = 8 - 5 = 3 (or 5 in some cases).

Protons are present in the nucleus of every atom. The number of protons in an atom determines its atomic number. The atomic number is denoted by the symbol 'Z' and is a fundamental property that uniquely identifies an element.

content.title: Definition and Significance
content.text: The atomic number (Z) is defined as the total number of protons present in the nucleus of an atom. All atoms of an element have the same atomic number. In fact, elements are defined and distinguished by the number of protons they possess. For example, hydrogen has Z = 1 (one proton), carbon has Z = 6 (six protons), oxygen has Z = 8 (eight protons).

In a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons, making the atom electrically neutral. The atomic number determines the position of an element in the periodic table and its chemical properties.

The mass of an atom is practically due to protons and neutrons alone, as electrons contribute negligibly to atomic mass (electron mass ≈ 1/2000 of proton mass). Protons and neutrons, both present in the nucleus, are collectively called nucleons. Therefore, the mass of an atom resides in its nucleus.

content.title: Definition and Calculation
content.text: The mass number (A) is defined as the sum of the total number of protons and neutrons present in the nucleus of an atom. Mass Number (A) = Number of Protons (Z) + Number of Neutrons (N), or A = Z + N. For example, carbon with 6 protons and 6 neutrons has mass number A = 6 + 6 = 12.
Example 1: Carbon-12: Number of protons = 6, Number of neutrons = 6, Mass number = 6 + 6 = 12. This isotope is written as ¹²₆C.
Example 2: Aluminium-27: Number of protons = 13, Number of neutrons = 14, Mass number = 13 + 14 = 27. This is written as ²⁷₁₃Al.

In the standard notation for an atom, the atomic number, mass number, and symbol of the element are written as: Mass Number (A) over Atomic Number (Z), with the element symbol on the right. For example, nitrogen is written as ¹⁴₇N, where 14 is the mass number and 7 is the atomic number.

In nature, atoms of some elements have been identified that have the same atomic number but different mass numbers. These atoms are called isotopes. Isotopes are atoms of the same element having the same atomic number but different mass numbers.

Example 1: Hydrogen Isotopes - Hydrogen has three isotopic forms: (i) Protium (¹₁H): 1 proton, 0 neutrons, mass number = 1. (ii) Deuterium (²₁H or D): 1 proton, 1 neutron, mass number = 2. (iii) Tritium (³₁H or T): 1 proton, 2 neutrons, mass number = 3. All have atomic number 1, but different mass numbers (1, 2, and 3 respectively).

Example 2: Carbon Isotopes - Carbon has two stable isotopes: (i) Carbon-12 (¹²₆C): 6 protons, 6 neutrons, mass number = 12. (ii) Carbon-14 (¹⁴₆C): 6 protons, 8 neutrons, mass number = 14. Both have atomic number 6 but different mass numbers.

Example 3: Chlorine Isotopes - Chlorine has two naturally occurring isotopes: (i) Chlorine-35 (³⁵₁₇Cl): 17 protons, 18 neutrons, mass number = 35. (ii) Chlorine-37 (³⁷₁₇Cl): 17 protons, 20 neutrons, mass number = 37. Both have atomic number 17 but different mass numbers.

content.title: Key Characteristics of Isotopes
content.text: Isotopes have: (i) Same atomic number (same number of protons). (ii) Different mass numbers (different number of neutrons). (iii) Same chemical properties (because valency depends on outermost electrons, which are the same). (iv) Different physical properties (because physical properties depend on mass and nuclear properties).

Many elements consist of a mixture of isotopes in nature. Each isotope of an element is a pure substance. When an element occurs in multiple isotopic forms, the mass we use is the average atomic mass, calculated based on the relative abundance of each isotope.

Example: Chlorine Atomic Mass - Chlorine occurs in nature in two isotopic forms: mass 35 u (chlorine-35) and mass 37 u (chlorine-37) in the ratio of 3:1 (or 75% and 25%). The average atomic mass is calculated as: Average mass = (35 × 75/100) + (37 × 25/100) = (35 × 0.75) + (37 × 0.25) = 26.25 + 9.25 = 35.5 u.

content.title: Understanding Average Atomic Mass
content.text: The average atomic mass of an element is the weighted average mass of all naturally occurring isotopes of that element. This does NOT mean that any single atom of the element has the average mass as a fractional value. Instead, if you take a sample of the element, it will contain a mixture of all isotopes, and the average mass represents the overall mass of the sample divided by the number of atoms.

If an element has no isotopes (i.e., occurs as a single stable form), then the mass of its atom would be the same as the sum of protons and neutrons in it. For example, if an element has 8 protons and 8 neutrons, its atomic mass would be exactly 16 u. However, if the element occurs in isotopic forms, the percentage of each isotopic form must be known to calculate the average mass accurately.

Although the chemical properties of all isotopes of an element are the same (because they have the same number of valence electrons), some isotopes have special properties that make them useful in various scientific and medical fields.

content.title: Important Applications
content.text: Some key applications of isotopes include: (i) Uranium-235: Used as fuel in nuclear reactors for generating electricity. (ii) Cobalt-60: Used in the treatment of cancer through radiation therapy. (iii) Iodine-131: Used in the treatment of goitre (thyroid disorder). These isotopes possess special radioactive or physical properties that make them valuable in their respective applications.

The use of isotopes in medicine and industry demonstrates how the nuclear properties of atoms can be harnessed for beneficial purposes. Radioactive isotopes are particularly useful because they emit radiation that can be detected and utilized for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes.

Isobars are atoms of different elements with different atomic numbers but the same mass number. They have the same total number of nucleons (protons + neutrons) but different numbers of protons and neutrons.

Example 1: Argon and Calcium - Argon (¹⁶₁₈Ar): atomic number = 18, mass number = 40 (18 protons + 22 neutrons). Calcium (⁴⁰₂₀Ca): atomic number = 20, mass number = 40 (20 protons + 20 neutrons). These atoms have the same mass number (40) but different atomic numbers (18 and 20), making them isobars.

content.title: Key Difference from Isotopes
content.text: Isotopes are atoms of the SAME element with different mass numbers (different neutrons, same protons). Isobars are atoms of DIFFERENT elements with the same mass number (different protons and neutrons, but same total nucleons). Isotopes have similar chemical properties; isobars have completely different chemical properties.

The distinction between isotopes and isobars is important in understanding atomic structure. While isotopes vary only in the number of neutrons, isobars involve atoms of completely different elements with different atomic numbers but coincidentally the same total mass.

The understanding of atomic structure evolved through the contributions of multiple scientists, each proposing models based on experimental evidence. This evolution demonstrates how scientific knowledge progresses through hypothesis, experimentation, and refinement.

content.title: Thomson's Model Summary
content.text: Thomson's Plum Pudding Model: (i) Atom has a positively charged sphere. (ii) Electrons are embedded throughout. (iii) Explains electrical neutrality. Limitation: Could not explain Rutherford's experimental results.

content.title: Rutherford's Model Summary
content.text: Rutherford's Nuclear Model: (i) Atom has a small, dense, positively charged nucleus. (ii) Electrons revolve in circular paths around the nucleus. (iii) Explains alpha-particle scattering results. Limitation: Could not explain atomic stability (electrons should fall into nucleus due to energy loss).

content.title: Bohr's Model Summary
content.text: Bohr's Model: (i) Electrons occupy discrete energy levels (shells K, L, M, N...). (ii) Electrons do not radiate energy while in these orbits. (iii) Each shell has maximum electron capacity. (iv) Explains atomic stability and chemical properties. Advantage: Most successful in explaining properties of first eighteen elements.

Bohr's model was the most successful in explaining atomic properties for simple atoms and is still used for educational purposes. However, modern quantum mechanics has further refined our understanding of electron behavior in atoms, treating electrons not as particles in fixed orbits but as probability distributions (orbitals) around the nucleus.

glossary.definition: The smallest unit of matter that retains the chemical properties of an element, consisting of a nucleus (protons and neutrons) surrounded by electrons in shells.
The total number of protons present in the nucleus of an atom. It uniquely identifies an element and determines its position in the periodic table.
The sum of the total number of protons and neutrons present in the nucleus of an atom. Formula: A = Z + N (where N is the number of neutrons).
A positively charged sub-atomic particle present in the nucleus of an atom. Mass = 1 u, Charge = +1. Represented as p⁺.
A negatively charged sub-atomic particle that orbits the nucleus. Mass ≈ 1/2000 u (negligible), Charge = -1. Represented as e⁻.
A neutral (uncharged) sub-atomic particle present in the nucleus of most atoms. Mass ≈ 1 u, Charge = 0. Represented as n. Absent in protium (¹₁H).
The small, dense, positively charged centre of an atom containing protons and neutrons. Contains nearly all the mass of the atom.
A collective term for protons and neutrons present in the nucleus. Both have mass ≈ 1 u.
A region around the nucleus where electrons can be located. Shells are designated as K, L, M, N, ... or n = 1, 2, 3, 4, ... Electrons in each shell have specific energy levels.
The electrons present in the outermost shell of an atom. These electrons determine the chemical properties and combining capacity (valency) of the atom.
The combining capacity of an atom, determined by the number of electrons gained, lost, or shared to achieve a stable configuration (usually an octet in the outermost shell).
A stable configuration in which the outermost shell of an atom contains 8 electrons. Atoms tend to achieve this configuration through chemical reactions.
Atoms of the same element (same atomic number) with different mass numbers (different numbers of neutrons). They have identical chemical properties but different physical properties.
Atoms of different elements (different atomic numbers) with the same mass number (same total number of nucleons). They have different chemical and physical properties.
The scheme for distributing electrons in different shells of an atom following the rules: maximum electrons = 2n², outermost shell max = 8, inner shells fill first.
A doubly-charged helium ion (He²⁺) consisting of 2 protons and 2 neutrons. Used by Rutherford in his famous gold foil experiment.
Positively charged radiations discovered by E. Goldstein in gas discharge tubes, which led to the discovery of protons.
Negatively charged rays discovered by J.J. Thomson, which led to the identification of electrons.
A standard unit for measuring the mass of atoms and sub-atomic particles. 1 u ≈ 1.66 × 10⁻²⁷ kg. Mass of proton ≈ mass of neutron ≈ 1 u.
The condition in which an atom has equal numbers of protons and electrons, resulting in zero net electrical charge.

summary_points: An atom consists of three fundamental sub-atomic particles: electrons (negatively charged, mass ≈ 1/2000 u), protons (positively charged, mass = 1 u), and neutrons (neutral, mass ≈ 1 u). Protons and neutrons are collectively called nucleons and reside in the nucleus.
The atomic number (Z) is the number of protons in an atom and uniquely identifies an element. In a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the atomic number.
The mass number (A) is the sum of protons and neutrons in the nucleus. Mass number = Atomic number + Number of neutrons, or A = Z + N.
Thomson proposed that electrons are embedded in a sphere of positive charge (plum pudding model), which explained electrical neutrality but could not explain experimental observations.

Rutherford's alpha-particle scattering experiment showed that atoms have a small, dense nucleus containing protons, with electrons orbiting in the surrounding space. This explained that most of the atom is empty space.
Rutherford's model proposed electrons revolving in circular orbits around the nucleus, but this model could not explain atomic stability (electrons should lose energy and fall into nucleus).

Bohr's model proposed that electrons occupy discrete energy levels (shells) designated as K, L, M, N, ... or n = 1, 2, 3, 4, .... Electrons do not radiate energy while in these allowed orbits, explaining atomic stability.
The Bohr-Bury scheme determines electron distribution in shells: (i) Maximum electrons per shell = 2n² (ii) Maximum in outermost shell = 8 (iii) Shells fill step-wise from innermost to outermost.

Valency is the combining capacity of an atom, determined by the number of electrons in the outermost shell. Atoms gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve a stable octet (8 electrons) in the outermost shell.
For atoms with ≤4 outermost electrons, valency = number of outermost electrons (lose/share). For atoms with >4 outermost electrons, valency = 8 - number of outermost electrons (gain/share).

Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same atomic number) with different mass numbers (different neutrons). They have identical chemical properties but different physical properties. Examples: protium (¹₁H), deuterium (²₁H), tritium (³₁H).
Isobars are atoms of different elements (different atomic numbers) with the same mass number. They have different chemical and physical properties. Example: Argon-40 (¹⁶₁₈Ar) and Calcium-40 (⁴⁰₂₀Ca).

Average atomic mass of an element with isotopes is calculated as a weighted average based on the relative abundance of each isotope. For example, Chlorine with 75% Cl-35 and 25% Cl-37 has average atomic mass = 35.5 u.

Many isotopes have practical applications: Uranium-235 in nuclear reactors, Cobalt-60 in cancer treatment, Iodine-131 in thyroid disease treatment. These applications utilize the special radioactive properties of these isotopes.

The evolution of atomic models (Thomson → Rutherford → Bohr) demonstrates how scientific knowledge progresses through experiments and refinement. Bohr's model was most successful in explaining properties of atoms with up to 18 electrons.
